section.title: The Peace Offering
section.sectionRationale: The third of the five offerings, the peace offering is the only one of the major sacrifices that the worshiper himself gets to eat, shared in a meal before the LORD. It continues the sacrificial law collection, showing that atonement leads not only to acceptance but to fellowship.
section.thesis: The peace offering, whether from the herd, the flock, or a goat, gives all the fat to God on the altar while the meat becomes a shared meal, teaching that reconciliation with God issues in genuine fellowship and celebration in His presence.
section.frameNote: Three movements, one for each kind of animal, herd, sheep, and goat, each ending in the same refrain: all the fat belongs to the LORD.

units.label: An animal from the herd
units.note: hands laid on the head, blood sprinkled

verses.text: “If one's offering is a {{shelamim|peace offering}} and he offers an animal from the herd, whether male or female, he must present it without blemish before the LORD.
He is to lay his hand on the head of the offering and slaughter it at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting. Then Aaron's sons the priests shall sprinkle the blood on all sides of the altar.
From the peace offering he is to bring an offering made by fire to the LORD: the {{chelev|fat}} that covers the entrails, all the fat that is on them,
both kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he is to remove with the kidneys.
Then Aaron's sons are to burn it on the altar atop the burnt offering that is on the burning wood, as an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
If, however, one's peace offering to the LORD is from the flock, he must present a male or female without blemish.
If he is presenting a lamb for his offering, he must present it before the LORD.
He is to lay his hand on the head of his offering and slaughter it in front of the Tent of Meeting. Then Aaron's sons shall sprinkle its blood on all sides of the altar.

And from the peace offering he shall bring an offering made by fire to the LORD consisting of its fat: the entire fat tail cut off close to the backbone, the fat that covers the entrails, all the fat that is on them,
both kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he is to remove with the kidneys.
Then the priest is to burn them on the altar as food, an offering made by fire to the LORD.

units.label: A goat
units.note: the same pattern, a permanent statute

verses.text: If one's offering is a goat, he is to present it before the LORD.
He must lay his hand on its head and slaughter it in front of the Tent of Meeting. Then Aaron's sons shall sprinkle its blood on all sides of the altar.
And from his offering he shall present an offering made by fire to the LORD: the fat that covers the entrails, all the fat that is on them,
both kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he is to remove with the kidneys.
Then the priest is to burn the food on the altar as an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma. All the fat is the LORD's.
This is a permanent statute for the generations to come, wherever you live: You must not eat any fat or any blood.”

shelamim.gloss: The peace offering, related to shalom, wholeness or well being. Unlike the burnt offering, the worshiper shares in eating this sacrifice, so it pictures a completed, restored relationship celebrated in a shared meal before God.

chelev.gloss: Fat, considered the richest and best part of the animal. Reserved exclusively for God and never to be eaten, verse 17, the fat represents giving the LORD the finest portion before anything is kept back for human use.

basic.context: The peace offering is the third of the five great sacrifices, and it stands apart because the worshiper actually eats most of the meat, sharing a celebratory meal in God's presence. Whatever the animal, an ox, a lamb, or a goat, the fat and inner organs always belong entirely to the LORD, burned on the altar, while the meat becomes food for a shared meal.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes Israel's actual old covenant sacrificial system, not a ritual for Christians to repeat today. Its abiding truth is that peace with God, once atonement is made, leads to genuine fellowship, joy, and shared celebration rather than mere formal acceptance.
basic.originalLanguages: Shelamim is related to shalom, meaning wholeness and well being, showing that this offering pictures a relationship restored to completeness. Chelev, fat, the richest part of the animal, always belongs to God alone, teaching that the best portion is reserved for Him before anything is kept for human enjoyment.
basic.typology: The shared meal, where the worshiper eats in God's presence, anticipates the fellowship believers now enjoy with God through Christ and looks forward to the marriage supper of the Lamb. The exclusive claim on the fat, the richest part, foreshadows giving God the very best rather than mere leftovers.
basic.christ: Christ is our peace, Ephesians 2:14, who has made peace through the blood of His cross, Colossians 1:20, and who now invites His people to feast with Him, both now at His table and finally at the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19:9.
basic.god: God does not merely tolerate sinners at a distance once atonement is made; He invites them to a shared meal in His presence, revealing His desire for genuine fellowship, not just formal reconciliation. His claim on the fat, the best part, shows He is not satisfied with token or leftover devotion.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 2:14 calls Christ our peace. Colossians 1:20 says God made peace through the blood of Christ's cross. Revelation 19:9 describes the marriage supper of the Lamb, the ultimate shared feast with God.
basic.oneStory: The peace offering shows that atonement is not the end of the story but the beginning of fellowship, a shared meal celebrating restored relationship with God. That pattern anticipates Christ, who is Himself our peace, and who invites His reconciled people to feast with Him now and forever at His table.

context.literary: The peace offering follows the burnt and grain offerings and completes the set of what are sometimes called the sweet savor offerings, those offered voluntarily and accepted as a pleasing aroma, in contrast to the sin and guilt offerings that follow, which deal specifically with sin.
context.historical: Communal meals at sanctuaries were common across the ancient Near East, but Israel's peace offering was distinct in requiring atoning blood first, laid hands, and the LORD's own claim on the fat before any human being ate a bite.
context.canonical: The peace offering's pattern of shared covenant meals runs throughout Scripture, from the elders eating before God on Sinai, Exodus 24:11, to the Lord's Supper, to the promised marriage supper of the Lamb.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To instruct Israel in an offering that, after atoning blood is applied and the fat given wholly to God, results in a shared, celebratory meal, teaching that reconciliation with God is meant to be enjoyed, not merely secured.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This describes an actual ritual meal for old covenant Israel; it does not prescribe animal sacrifice or ritual meals for the church. Its abiding principle is that restored fellowship with God is meant to be experienced with joy, not treated only as a legal transaction.

debates.question: Why is fat singled out as permanently forbidden to eat, verse 17, a rule repeated elsewhere in Leviticus?

views.label: Fat belongs exclusively to God as the richest portion
views.case: The text presents fat as reserved for the altar precisely because it is the best part, teaching that the finest portion of any gift belongs to God first.

views.label: A practical and symbolic separation from pagan practice
views.case: Some scholars note that reserving fat for the deity was distinct from surrounding cultures' practices and served to mark Israel's worship as set apart, though the text itself grounds the command in giving God the best, not merely cultural distinction.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the same underlying principle, that the finest portion of what Israel had was to be surrendered to God rather than kept for human consumption.

study.originalLanguages: Shelamim shares its root with shalom, wholeness and peace, marking this as the offering of restored relationship celebrated in a meal. The chelev, fat, always belongs to the LORD alone, since it was considered the richest part of the animal, teaching Israel to give God the best portion first.

typology.type: The shared meal eaten in God's presence
typology.fulfillment: Fellowship with God through Christ now, and the marriage supper of the Lamb to come, Revelation 19:9.

typology.type: The fat, the richest portion, given wholly to God
typology.fulfillment: Giving God the best of what we have, not merely what is left over, echoed in the call to offer our bodies as living sacrifices, Romans 12:1.

study.christ: Ephesians 2:14 declares that Christ Himself is our peace, having broken down the dividing wall of hostility, and Colossians 1:20 says God made peace through the blood of His cross. The peace offering's shared meal, celebrated only after atoning blood was applied, anticipates the fellowship believers now have with God through Christ's blood, and the future feast at the marriage supper of the Lamb.
study.god: God desires more than formal acceptance from His people; He invites them into shared celebration and fellowship once atonement has been made. His claim on the fat, the richest part of the offering, reveals a God who is not satisfied with leftover devotion but calls His people to give Him their very best.

crossReferences.note: Christ Himself is our peace, breaking down the dividing wall of hostility between God and His people.
God made peace through the blood of Christ's cross, reconciling all things to Himself.
The marriage supper of the Lamb, the ultimate shared feast celebrating restored fellowship with God.

study.oneStory: The peace offering teaches that reconciliation with God through atoning blood is meant to lead to genuine, joyful fellowship, pictured in a shared meal where the worshiper eats before the LORD. That pattern points forward to Christ, our peace, who reconciles His people to God and invites them to feast with Him now and at the marriage supper of the Lamb to come.

quiz.q: What distinguishes the peace offering from the burnt offering?

options.text: Most of the meat becomes food for a shared meal, rather than being wholly burned.
options.note: Only the fat and inner organs are burned, verses 3 to 5; the rest becomes a shared meal, unlike the burnt offering which is wholly consumed.

options.text: No blood is applied to the altar.
options.note: Verses 2, 8, and 13 all specify that blood is sprinkled on all sides of the altar, just as with the burnt offering.

options.text: It can only be offered by priests.
options.note: The chapter describes any Israelite worshiper bringing this offering, not only priests.

options.text: There is no meaningful difference.
options.note: The shared meal is the distinguishing feature that sets this offering apart from the burnt offering.

quiz.q: What three animals from the herd, flock, and beyond does this chapter permit for the peace offering?

options.text: An animal from the herd, a lamb from the flock, and a goat.
options.note: The three sections of the chapter, verses 1, 6 to 7, and 12, describe exactly these three categories.

options.text: Only doves and pigeons.
options.note: Birds are permitted for the burnt and sin offerings for the poor, but this chapter describes herd, flock, and goat animals.

options.text: Only bulls.
options.note: The chapter describes more than bulls alone, including lambs and goats.

options.text: No animals are specified.
options.note: The chapter gives detailed specifications for three categories of animals.

quiz.q: What part of the peace offering is always burned on the altar and never eaten by anyone?

options.text: The fat, including the fat covering the entrails and around the kidneys and liver.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4, 9 to 10, and 14 to 16 all specify this fat as belonging entirely to the LORD.

options.text: The meat of the shoulder.
options.note: The shoulder is not the portion specified here as reserved for the altar; the fat is.

options.text: The skin and hide.
options.note: The chapter concerns the fat portions, not the hide.

options.text: Nothing is reserved; the whole animal is eaten.
options.note: Verse 16 explicitly states that all the fat belongs to the LORD.

quiz.q: What permanent statute closes this chapter, verse 17?

options.text: You must not eat any fat or any blood.
options.note: Verse 17 states this prohibition as a permanent statute for all generations.

options.text: You must offer a peace offering every day.
options.note: No daily requirement for the peace offering is stated here; it was a voluntary offering.

options.text: Only priests may offer peace offerings.
options.note: The chapter describes any Israelite bringing this offering, not priests exclusively.

options.text: No lasting statute is given.
options.note: Verse 17 explicitly states a permanent statute.

quiz.q: What does the Hebrew word for peace offering, shelamim, relate to?

options.text: Shalom, meaning wholeness, well being, and peace.
options.note: The shared root points to restored relationship and completeness as the offering's meaning.

options.text: A word meaning silence or stillness.
options.note: This is not the root meaning connected to shelamim.

options.text: A word meaning fire or burning.
options.note: That concept relates more to olah, burnt offering, not shelamim.

options.text: The word has no connection to any other Hebrew concept.
options.note: Shelamim shares a clear root with shalom, peace and wholeness.

questions: The peace offering meant sharing an actual meal with joy in God's presence after atonement was made. Does your relationship with God feel more like a formal transaction or a genuine, joyful fellowship?
God claimed the fat, the richest part, before anything else was eaten. What is the best part of your time, money, or attention that you tend to keep for yourself rather than give to God first?

This offering could come from the herd, the flock, or a goat, showing that God welcomed worship from whatever a person had. Do you hold back from worship because you feel you do not have enough to offer?

The peace offering pictures the completed relationship, shalom, that reconciliation with God brings. Where in your life do you need to receive the peace Christ has already won rather than continuing to strive for it?
This meal anticipates the marriage supper of the Lamb, a feast still to come. How does looking forward to that future feast change the way you view struggles or losses in the present?

translationNotes.note: Peace offering translates shelamim, sharing its root with shalom; some translations render it fellowship offering to emphasize the shared meal that distinguishes it from the wholly burned offerings.

The entire fat tail cut off close to the backbone refers to the broad, fatty tail of certain Middle Eastern sheep breeds, considered a delicacy and therefore reserved specifically for God rather than human consumption.
This prohibition on eating fat and blood is called a permanent statute, chuqqat olam, distinguishing it from ceremonial regulations tied specifically to the tabernacle and priesthood.
